text: VO2max declines approximately 10% per decade in both men and women regardless of physical activity level.
High-intensity exercise may reduce the age-related VO2max decline by up to 50% in young and middle-aged men if maintained long term, but not in older men.
specific_value: up to 50% reduction in decline rate

text: Middle-aged and older women do not appear able to reduce VO2max loss rates below 10% per decade, possibly related to estrogen status.
Maintaining high-intensity training is limited to approximately one decade at best and to a select few individuals.
Age-related VO2max decline occurs non-linearly, with accelerated loss in the twenties and thirties for sedentary individuals and upon cessation of training for athletic individuals.
The decline in VO2max is primarily driven by reductions in maximal heart rate and lean body mass, both central and peripheral factors.
Exercise training does not influence the age-related decline in maximal heart rate, but lean body mass can be partially maintained through exercise including strength training.
Individuals over age 70 demonstrate accelerated non-linear VO2max loss rates beyond the typical 10% per decade.
